_meta.description: mtDNA haplogroup diagnostic mutations and mitochondrial coupling classification. Based on Doug Wallace's mitochondrial paradigm. Diagnostic mutations from MITOMAP/PhyloTree Build 17.
_meta.caveat: The coupling/uncoupling classification is a research framework supported by cybrid studies and epidemiological data, but not universally accepted. Individual variation within haplogroups is large.

references.desc: Wallace 2015 (Cell) — Mitochondrial DNA variation in human radiation and disease
Mishmar/Wallace 2003 (PNAS) — Natural selection shaped regional mtDNA variation
Ruiz-Pesini/Wallace 2004 (Science) — Purifying and adaptive selection on mtDNA
Gomez-Duran 2010 — Cybrid OXPHOS differences H vs T
Kenney/Wallace 2014 — African vs European haplogroup bioenergetics

L0.etc: Complex I+IV: tight coupling, high ATP yield, low heat. Most ancient human lineage (~150 kya)

L1.etc: Complex IV COI Y496H substitution. Tightly coupled, efficient OXPHOS

L2.etc: Higher mtDNA-encoded gene expression vs European cybrids

L3.etc: Ancestor of all non-African lineages. Transitional coupling. ~70 kya

M.etc: Out-of-Africa southern route (~60 kya). Complex I 10400T. Descendants diversify: tropical M coupled, arctic C/D/G less coupled

N.etc: Out-of-Africa northern route. Reverts ND3 10398G→A and ATP6 8701G→A from L/M ancestral state

R.etc: Parent of most European haplogroups (H, J, T, U, K) and Asian B, F

A.etc: ATP6 variation in arctic lineages. Native American + Northeast Siberian

B.etc: Classic 9bp deletion at 8271-8289 (not yet representable in the current mutation schema). Polynesian, Pacific coastal. Studied in altitude adaptation contexts; specific HAPE signal is weaker than nuclear variants like ADH7

D.etc: 5178A classic marker. Greater non-shivering thermogenesis. Associated with longevity in Japan

E.etc: M descendant in tropical SE Asia. Retains M-type 10400T

F.etc: N descendant. Associated with sprint/power athletics (efficient ATP burst)

G.etc: M descendant. Siberia, Ainu, northeast Asia. Cold-adapted

H.etc: Most common European haplogroup (~40%). The rCRS IS haplogroup H. Higher mtDNA copy number, higher ROS vs Uk cybrids. Post-glacial Franco-Cantabrian expansion

I.etc: Complex I (10398G convergent with M) + Complex III (15043A). Multiple ETC hits → reduced coupling

J.etc: Canonical uncoupled haplogroup. Multiple Complex I hits: ND3 10398G, ND5 13708A, ND1 4216C. Lower ATP, higher heat. LHON background. Associated with longevity (reduced ROS)

K.etc: Subclade of U8. ATP6 9055A affects ATP synthase. Complex III 14798C. Common in Ashkenazi Jewish populations

T.etc: Shares JT-cluster markers with J. ND2 4917G Complex I. Lower Complex I+IV activity vs H in cybrids. Lower sperm motility. Better oxidative stress survival

U.etc: Ancient European lineage (~45 kya). U5 dominant in Mesolithic hunter-gatherers. Higher thermogenic capacity. K is subclade (U8)

V.etc: Sister to H. ND2 4580A Complex I. Post-glacial re-expansion. High frequency in Sami (~40%) and Basques

W.etc: ATP6 8994A (Complex V) + ND2 5460A (Complex I). Near East, South Asia, Finland

X.etc: COI 6221C+6371T (Complex IV) + ND5/ND6 Complex I hits. Bimodal: Near East + Native American (Algonquian, Sioux)

Z.etc: M8 descendant (shares ancestry with C). Sami, Finns, Central Asians, Siberia

H1.etc: Post-LGM expansion clade. ~25% of Iberian mtDNA, ~13% W. European H. Sepsis-survival advantage in ICU cohorts (Baudouin 2010, PMID 18852894)

H3.etc: Sister of H1 in Franco-Cantabrian post-glacial expansion. Protective signal for ischemic cardiomyopathy and Parkinson's in Spanish cohorts (Pereira 2005, PMID 16401464)

J1.etc: J1c sub-clade enriched in centenarians (Italian, Irish cohorts). LHON 11778 penetrance modifier — elevates vision-loss risk in 11778G>A carriers (Hudson 2007, PMID 19234490)

J2.etc: Higher LHON 14484 penetrance than J1. MS susceptibility modifier in some European cohorts. Less common than J1

K1.etc: Most common K branch in Europe (~5%). K1a1b1a sub-sub-clade is the dominant Ashkenazi Jewish founder lineage (~32% of AJ mtDNA, Behar 2006 PMID 16404693)

T1.etc: T1a peaks in Eastern Europe and Caucasus (~3-8%). Asthenozoospermia association (sperm motility differential — Ruiz-Pesini 2000 originally on T, refined to T1)

T2.etc: Dominant T sub-clade in Europe (~75% of all T). T2b especially common in British Isles. Reported AD/PD signals in some cohorts — evidence weakly replicated, treat with caution

U5a.etc: Mesolithic European hunter-gatherer founder. U5a1a1 over-represented in Italian centenarians (Raule 2014). Vitamin D synthesis efficiency hypothesis — lighter pigmentation co-evolved on U5 backgrounds

U5b.origin: Mesolithic Europe — oldest verified European mtDNA branch
U5b.etc: Oldest dated European lineage (~30 kya, La Braña hunter-gatherer). Up to ~50% of Saami mtDNA; ~30% of Finns. Strong cold-adaptation signal

U6.origin: North Africa (back-migration from Near East ~30 kya)
U6.etc: Defining mtDNA of indigenous North African populations (~10-20% in Berber groups; ~30% Canary Islands Guanches). Distinct from U5 — separate Levantine back-migration lineage

A2.etc: One of the four founding Native American mtDNA lineages. Coastal Pacific migration route. Highest frequencies in Na-Dene (~30%) and Eskimo-Aleut (~70%) populations

coupled.label: Coupled (efficient ATP, low heat)
coupled.description: Tightly coupled electron transport chain — maximizes ATP yield per calorie with minimal heat generation. Evolved in warm equatorial environments.
coupled.implications: Higher UV and warmth requirements. May struggle with cold environments or low-light conditions at northern latitudes. Prioritize sun exposure, warm climate, and circadian rhythm alignment.

coupled_intermediate.label: Coupled to intermediate
coupled_intermediate.description: Moderately tight coupling with some metabolic flexibility. Adapted to tropical and warm temperate zones.
coupled_intermediate.implications: Good sun and warmth tolerance. Some cold tolerance but not optimized for high latitudes.

intermediate.label: Intermediate
intermediate.description: Balanced coupling — the transitional state from the out-of-Africa dispersal. Metabolically flexible.
intermediate.implications: Adaptable to a range of environments. Neither strongly cold-adapted nor strongly heat-adapted.

intermediate_uncoupled.label: Intermediate to uncoupled
intermediate_uncoupled.description: Trending toward uncoupled. Evolved in cold or high-altitude environments. Higher thermogenic capacity.
intermediate_uncoupled.implications: Better cold tolerance. May benefit from cold exposure practices. Higher caloric needs in warm climates.

mildly_uncoupled.label: Mildly uncoupled
mildly_uncoupled.description: Slightly loose electron transport coupling. Core European temperate adaptation. Moderate heat generation.
mildly_uncoupled.implications: Well-suited to temperate climates. Moderate cold tolerance. Standard caloric needs.

moderately_uncoupled.label: Moderately uncoupled
moderately_uncoupled.description: Noticeably loose coupling with higher heat generation per calorie. Cold-adapted with reduced ATP efficiency.
moderately_uncoupled.implications: Good cold tolerance. May benefit from cold exposure. Higher caloric turnover. Consider caloric needs if living in warm climates.

uncoupled.label: Uncoupled (more heat, less ATP)
uncoupled.description: Loosely coupled electron transport — generates significantly more heat per calorie at the expense of ATP production. Historically advantageous in cold northern climates. Multiple Complex I variants reduce coupling efficiency.
uncoupled.implications: Naturally thermogenic — tolerates cold well. Lower ATP per calorie means higher caloric needs. May have reduced ROS (associated with longevity). Consider cold exposure as aligned with mitochondrial phenotype.
